sentences.text: Yes.
As I told before, that I went to a Polish high school.

But by the time I joined the organization Hashomer Hatzair, I started to lose interest in my friends, which I had before, because they, you see, as we grew older, the girls, they started to have interest in dresses and boys and that, which my mind went into a different direction.
I was very serious.
We were trying to solve the ills of the whole world then.
So [LAUGHS] so I was not interested in gossips with the other girls.
And so when I was in the fifth grade, I decided to switch schools and go to the Jewish school, the Jewish high school, where all my friends from all different groups of Zionist movements were.
You know?
And where we learned Hebrew, which I knew a little bit before, because we studied Hebrew in our organization as well.
And, but we had Hebrew.
We had all the subject, subjects like literature, Jewish, and Hebrew, and Talmud, and everything else.
And of course, the normal subjects as every normal public school in Poland had.
And I was very happy there.
We had all the Jewish teachers.
As it was quite a small town, we knew each other from home and from before.
Some were even friends of my eldest siblings, brother and sister.
And it was-- first of all, it was a very high standard school.
We had to keep a high standard, to keep the permission to have a Jewish school.
So and I finished there, having those two exams, one for the Hebrew and one for the-- all the other subjects.
I finished very well, with very high degrees.
And I was ready to, to go on my way to university.
I still did not know what I want.
Like, my sister was definite that she wants to be a doctor, and my brother to be an engineer.
I still did know not what I want.
What I wanted to be, really, a teacher in a very young children's school.
That was really what I liked to do.
But as I told you, even if I would finished, it was very problematic if I would get a job in Poland.
Anyway, I finished the school. 1932?
And first of all, there were-- it was not so cheap to keep kids in foreign countries on studies.
And then the pressure on my parents, who started to get older, especially my father, was-- started to get bigger and bigger.
And he started to complain from all those children, nobody's at home.
Nobody can help me.
And I saw really that he was in-- how [INAUDIBLE] [? will ?] [? tell ?] [? you-- ?] very low, because he could not understand the change.
They were brought up and born in Austrian time when the Jews did not have any pressure on them, and where it was really a rather free life.
So that was very hard.

So I started to take over the administration of the properties, the bookkeeping, and the business, and especially taxation, which was then a very big problem for the Jews, because the pressure was-- started to be very on.
And so I stayed at home.
And--
Oh, no.
That was-- we had a very big yard.
Then we had a shop, and then we had-- how I tell you-- the place where my father was manufacturing those things.
Very close, everything together.
By then, at the beginning, we lived-- till I was around 14 years old, we lived in the Jewish quarter next to, as I told you, next to the synagogue.
The yard was not far.
The whole town was not so big, so everything was really close.
When we-- I was around 14, we moved to the newer town, a newer part of the town, which was not specifically Jewish, but as I told you, the center, the new and the old, was mostly Jewish.
But still, it was not far, because in Przemysl, you did not need-- I don't speak about cars.
I think that there were only two cars altogether, and they were taken out only on special occasion.
But everywhere we went by foot, anyway.
We spent, for example, I-- we were swimming, yes?
We were swimming in the river, but we did not like the river in town.
So we were walking up to the countryside, around five kilometers from the town, because it was a beautiful place.
But there was no beach, or no nothing.
It is just [INAUDIBLE] fields, and-- so we were swimming there.
So we went in the morning, five kilometers there.
We were swimming.
We came back, five kilometers.
The same thing was with skating in winter.
We had an ice rink in the center of the town with electric lights at night for kids.
We went to school.
So we had to go in the afternoon when it was dark, but we did not use this ice skating rink.
We did not like it.
We went the opposite-- to the other side of the river.
Something like six, seven kilometers, was a, a, a man who owned a garden.
And he had a pond which was frozen stiff in the winter.
And he built a shack over there.
So we were going there to skating.
There, the few kilometers, skating there, and back the few kilometers.
So it was rather fun, because we were all young and healthy, and, you know, and it was much nicer than sit always in the center of the, of the town.
So, we stayed-- as I told, we, I stayed at home until 1939.
My eldest sister was by then married, and she lived with her family in another town, most to the east in Ukrainian part.
My brother married, stayed in Przemysl.
Joseph stayed in Przemysl.
He married, had a child, and was in business.
Rosa came back from her study and the problem started all over again, because she could not practice medicine in Prague.
She had to sign that she's, she's not going to stay there.
Yes?
But when she come to Poland, she was not allowed, because she had a foreign diploma.
There was a possibility to get through a special exam at the university, but the Jews were not admitted.
Even when my father wanted to pay and to bribe or something, it could not be-- just was not done, not at this time, anymore.
So she stayed at home, and she married.
In '38, she married.
[PAUSES]
And, that was-- and David was, of course, in France.
And the youngest was everywhere, because he was so undisciplined [LAUGHS] that he was, was sent from one school to another all over Poland.
And so I was the only one stayed, which stayed at home.
By then, I was in close friendship.
I met my husband, future husband, and we had a very nice group, this young people.
And as I told you, the life in Przemysl, at this time, was quite nice and-- for us, without problem, personal problem.
For me personally, or for the family?
Look.
When I look back, I can't understand how stupid we were.
[PAUSES] In '37, I think, '38, the first Jews were thrown out of Germany, especially those-- the Polish, the citizens who lived in Germany.
They were thrown out, and they, they come to Poland.
And they were telling us the, the horror stories, and nobody believed it.
And so much that even my parents, my father could sell everything, and we had the possibility to go away, he just looked around, and he told, I worked a whole life, and I'm not going to leave that.
Well, we were so much innocent, that in '39, in August, we went for a holiday.
My future husband, his friend, we were a group of 10 young people.
We went to a beautiful place in the mountains.
And the first September, when the first, the first day of war, we only woke up and we came home back.
That is why I'm telling, when you look back, how could-- couldn't you see all the signs and all the dangers?
But we didn't.
We were just [PAUSES] waiting for the inevitable.
You see?
We were sitting there like real sitting ducks.
[PAUSES] So in '39-- so of course, we heard about [? election ?] of Austria, we have-- of Czechoslovakia, and we still did not move.
[PAUSES] And in the first September '39, [PAUSES] the German crossed to Poland.
[PAUSES] In no time, as you know, it was a blitzkrieg.
In no time, they were in Przemysl.
We had the first bombing.
And so there were-- they did not stay long in Przemysl.
They were only a few days.
But in those few days, they had enough time to round up 500 most-- the most prominent Jewish citizens and shoot them on the spot.
[PAUSES] After those few days, they retreated to the left bank, as I told you, because the Russian came to take over our town.
[PAUSES] But that is how they started, and we were still staying in the town.
No.
We were not.
You know, at the beginning of the war, there was such a disarray in Poland.
And the-- it was the government or not the government.
We don't know.
They started to call on the all young people to go east.
East.
And the army, of course, which lost the war, was going east.
So the young people, young men, have to go east.
So we went.
My one brother, the youngest brother, the, the brother-in-law, my husband Stazik and his friend.
And then my mother looked at me, and told, look, there are five men.
One girl has to be with them to do something for them.
And as I was always the scout, hiking and always the one on the move, she told me, you are going with them.
And I went with them.
We travelled by foot.
Afterwards, by a horse-drawn carriage from Lwow, till we finished in [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], it is a very small shtetl on the-- in the corner between Russia and Romania.
And we had the best intention to cross to Romania, where my brother-in-law had a family, very close family.
But then, that day-- it was the 17 of September-- we heard the Russian are coming.
So of course, everybody was very happy.
And I, the only socialist in the whole family, was lying on the bed and crying.
[LAUGHS] And I told them, we have to go, because somehow, I did not expect the German, but I, I was afraid of the Russian.
And I told them, we have to go over the border.
But they did not want, because my brother left a wife with a small child.
My brother-in-law left my sister, who was pregnant then.
So they wanted to go back.
So we came back.
But then, we were told about those 500.
My father was in Przemysl.
[PAUSES] My father was in Przemysl, and that was a funny thing, because when they come to the house, they were looking for my brother-in-law.
And my father was going down to the janitor's to get the-- and the German met him on the stairs.
[PAUSES] You see, my father did not look very Jewish.
He was tall.
He was very handsome.
And the-- and the German, if somebody did not point-- if he hasn't got a beard, and somebody did not point to [? that, ?] they did not recognize [INAUDIBLE].
So they passed my father and asked him where [?
Babat ?] lives.
But my brother-in-law was not there at home, because he was with us over there.
But then, of course, we saw the Russian cross the border, because we were on the border.
Yes.
So we were going back to our town.
We-- and I will tell you that we still-- we were going through the Ukrainian villages and the Polish villages, and nothing happened.
They were not [INAUDIBLE].
They were friendly.
They gave us eat-- to eat.
They gave us shelter, and we still felt safe.
So we come back--
Where?
Ah, well, next day, when the Russian were coming, we started to go back, which did not take it.
Of course, we could not take a train to-- train stopped.
We had to go by any means we could.
So we came to Lwow.
And from Lwow, I could not even tell you how we went back.
And there in Lwow, my brother-in-law had family where we stopped.
[PAUSES] And somebody came from Przemysl and told him not to go back, [PAUSES] because the Russian started.
Now they're cleansing.
And their cleansing was the political [PAUSES] activists and [PAUSES] prominent people, rich people, all the government officials.
And that.
And as my brother-in-law was a very prominent Zionist, in the right movement, like today Likud.
And-- [PAUSES] and he came from a very prominent Jewish family.
He was one-- he was in danger.
So they told him not to go there.
But he wanted to go to see what his father, who was the [? president ?] of the Jewish council in Przemysl, if-- what happened to him, and, of course, his wife, who is pregnant in the last months.
So he-- we all come back.
The first day, when he went to the hairdresser-- to the barber, rather-- the [INAUDIBLE] came there, took him away, and we never saw him again.
[PAUSES]
Apparently, two months later, he was taken with all the political prisoners and disappeared.
[PAUSES] That was [?
Russia ?] time.
Then they started on us.
They nationalized everything.
It means they just took away all the properties, all the business, even the furniture, the best-- better furniture.
They divided every bigger apartment.
You see, in Poland, they were built like that, that usually there was another-- one entrance from the kitchen, from the yard, and another entrance were to the salon and to the other rooms.
So they divided each apartment on so many parts, as many entrances there were.
So they left us only to-- the smallest two rooms with a kitchen, which you can approach only from the back yard.
[PAUSES]
And one night, [PAUSES] I think it was [PAUSES] around [PAUSES] March or February.
It was still very cold.
They came at night, [PAUSES] and we knew that they are rounding up people.
They're rounding up, again, richer people, and especially those who came from the other side, you know, because the Jews started to flee from the German side.
And they stopped in Przemysl, because it was quite [INAUDIBLE].
So they had to register.
So they were rounding up those what they called [NON-ENGLISH], that was the-- who fled.
And [PAUSES] so when they started to, to, to arrest, always in the middle of the night, when they arrested the richer, nationalized people, [? this, ?] my father was ready.
And we were sitting.
And when we heard at night those heavy boots on the staircase, we were sitting there ready.
But they missed us, [PAUSES] and they went up.
And then the janitor's wife came crying that they came for Rosa.
She was the wife of a political prisoner.
And that was the system in Russia, that they arrested all the families of the political prisoners.
On the second floor, above us.
[PAUSES] And they took, this night, I went-- so I went up.
I was always in the middle of everything, because I did not want my parents to send them, because they could gather the parents together with her, you know?
So I went up to have a look what, if she needs a help, and she needed, because she was all alone with a three-month-old baby.
And-- [PAUSES] so I helped her to pack a few things.
[PAUSES] And at one stage, for example, one of those Russians saw a fur coat lying.
And he told-- we did not know where they are taking her.
That was the beginning now of our knowledge about Russia.
So he looked at the fur, and he told her, I think that better you take that with you.
You will need it where you are going.
[PAUSES]
So they took her to Siberia, to Kazakhstan.
[PAUSES] We got letters from her.
Nine months of snow, and that she was in a camp.
Very hard, with a three-month-old baby in the middle of winter.
[PAUSES]
And she was working very hard.
I don't know what they were doing there, manual work, something.
No, I can't tell you.
I told you, it was names, even what-- [PAUSES] where I was, I just, I'm hopeless.
So I know that it was Kazakhstan.
But she had a diploma.
They had diploma with her.
That is another story where I was involved, how she got the diploma.
But she had to have diploma.
And after six months, they transferred her to the-- a small town not far from the camp, where was a hospital.
And she worked as a doctor there till '41, when the war broke out.
It was Germany.
And the-- all the nationalities started to organize their liberation armies in Russia.
[PAUSES] She was not admitted to-- of course, not to the Polish army-- so she-- but she was admitted as a doctor to the Czech army.
She had the Czech diploma.
And she went through the whole war with the army as a doctor until till liberation of Prague.
That was her story.
In meantime, I was always in Przemysl.
I didn't move.
We were working, [PAUSES] my husband and I.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
[PAUSES] When my sister was taken, my parents were still there.
My brother, with his wife, and [? this ?] who lived next to us in the same, the same building, you know, that is next.
He left, because they took away his business.
And so he went to Lwow.
To live.
[PAUSES] And after that, when they cleansed the town from all those political and--
Yeah.
They started on those nationalized [NON-ENGLISH], nationalized people.
It means Przemysl was a border town.
You had to have a special pass to live there.
The pass had the special stamp, and people like my parents could not get the stamp.
They were the enemies of the state, because they were the [PAUSES] rich people.
So they have to leave.
And at least 100 kilometers from the town, and that was Lwow, was the place.
And I wanted to live with them, of course.
I packed all my things [? and ?] [? that. ?] And then my husband told, you know-- look, we knew each other five years anyway before that.
Sooner or later, we would be married.
So we are going to get married, [PAUSES] and so we did.
So I stayed in Przemysl.
I married my husband, changed my name to Rosenman, and, [PAUSES] and there we stayed.
I-- my parents left to Lwow.
And if you want to know how the Russian system worked, I will tell you one small thing.
I went to visit my parents once, and then I needed to see a doctor or a specialist.
I went the second time.
The third time, when I come to Lwow, my father was waiting for me in the railway station.
I saw a young man following us.
I did not take any notice.
I don't want to, to sound this, but I was used to it.
You know, so a man follows us.
We take a tram, he takes a tram.
We came out next to our house, he comes out.
He has the key from the house.
He opens, and he goes and stops us.
And he looks at me, and he tells me, why you are coming from the border town to Lwow so often?
So I told him, look.
My parents live here.
And I just come to visit them.
You are three times here from the border.
[PAUSES] That was suspicious.
And in a town like-- city.
It was not a town, Lwow.
It was more than a million people.
Everybody, they knew everybody's movement.
He knew that I was three times there.
Anyway, [PAUSES] we lived like that till '41.
Yes?
In '41, June 21, at night, we could hear shots.
I woke up my husband, and I told him, something is happening.
So he think so that oh, it is a skirmish, only a border skirmish.
I told him, no.
It looks like something more [PAUSES] important, especially when we saw the Russian started to run.
The soldiers, the officers started to run.
They did not know what to do.
And of course, it two hours took the German to cross the bridge.
And they were on our side.
Good.
